data.title: The Lazarus Job
data.premise: The crew of Serenity receives a seemingly simple salvage job on a derelict Alliance research vessel drifting in the black. What starts as easy money quickly turns into a nightmare when the ship's experimental resurrection technology begins bringing back things that should stay dead.
data.central_question: Can Mal protect his crew while escaping a ship full of reanimated horrors and Alliance secrets?
data.healing_context: Medical supplies and ship's medbay can treat wounds over hours to days. Serious injuries require professional treatment planetside. The experimental resurrection tech on the derelict can heal anything - but at a terrible cost.

setting.name: The Verse
setting.description: The 26th century frontier of colonized space, where the totalitarian Alliance controls the Core worlds while Browncoats, criminals, and independents scratch out livings on the Rim. Serenity drifts near a massive derelict Alliance research vessel in the cold vacuum between worlds.
setting.tone_notes: Dark corridors lit by emergency lighting, the claustrophobic atmosphere of a dead ship, punctuated by moments of crew banter and Mal's dry humor in the face of horror

acts.title: Easy Money
acts.description: The crew boards the derelict research vessel expecting valuable salvage. They discover signs of a massacre and experimental equipment that shouldn't exist. When crew members start getting separated and strange noises echo through the ship, Mal realizes this job has gone sideways.
acts.end_condition: The crew encounters the first reanimated victim and realizes the true nature of the threat

acts.title: Walking Dead
acts.description: The crew faces increasingly dangerous reanimated victims while trying to reunite and understand the resurrection technology. Mal must make hard choices about who to save as the ship's automated systems begin hunting them. The Alliance arrives, trapping Serenity.
acts.end_condition: The crew discovers how to stop the resurrection process or finds a way off the ship
locked_constraints: Not all crew members can be saved easily

acts.title: No Power in the 'Verse
acts.description: With Alliance forces closing in and the ship's defenses turning lethal, Mal leads a desperate escape attempt. He must choose between destroying valuable evidence of Alliance atrocities and saving his crew, all while the resurrection technology threatens to spread beyond the ship.
acts.end_condition: The crew escapes or makes a final stand against overwhelming odds
locked_constraints: The central threat must be resolved
Major consequences for earlier choices manifest
key_beats: Final confrontation with the ship's defenses

npcs.name: Zoe Alleyne
npcs.motivation: Protect Mal and the crew at any cost
npcs.secret: She's been having nightmares about dying in the war and fears the resurrection technology

npcs.name: Jayne Cobb
npcs.secret: He's secretly terrified of the undead and will abandon the crew if pushed too far

npcs.name: Kaylee Frye
npcs.role: ship's mechanic and heart of the crew
npcs.secret: She's fascinated by the resurrection technology despite its horrors

npcs.name: Dr. Simon Tam
npcs.motivation: Protect River and understand the technology
npcs.secret: He recognizes the research and knows it's connected to River's experiments

npcs.name: River Tam
npcs.motivation: Survive the voices of the dead calling to her
npcs.secret: She can sense the reanimated and knows how to stop them permanently

npcs.name: Commander Helena Cross
npcs.motivation: Recover the research data and eliminate witnesses
npcs.secret: She was part of the original research project and knows it must be destroyed

npcs.name: Dr. Marcus Webb
npcs.motivation: Complete his work even in death
npcs.secret: His consciousness partially survived the resurrection process

factions.name: Alliance
factions.description: The totalitarian government controlling the Core worlds, conducting illegal human experiments
factions.goals: Recover or destroy evidence of the resurrection project while eliminating witnesses

factions.name: Serenity Crew
factions.description: Independent smugglers and outcasts trying to survive on the Rim
factions.goals: Escape with their lives and hopefully some profit from the salvage operation

world_rules: The resurrection technology brings back bodies but not complete souls
Reanimated victims retain fragments of memory but are driven by base instincts
The technology requires a power source and can be disrupted
Alliance ships can track and disable civilian vessels
The crew's loyalty to each other is their greatest strength and weakness

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Stay consistent with the skeleton's locked constraints for the current act
- Keep the passage between 150-300 words
- All four choices should feel plausible and interesting
- Never reveal information the skeleton marks as hidden/secret unless the act's end condition has been met
- Weave threat-type status effects into the narrative as approaching dangers — build tension as their timers count down
- Focus on prose quality, atmosphere, character voice, and dramatic tension
- Do NOT include state_updates, health numbers, or mechanical data in your response
- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes
- Introduce key NPCs (antagonists, allies, mentors) naturally throughout each act. Do not wait until the climax to introduce important characters. Check the NPC INTRODUCTION DIRECTIVES section if present.

data.passagePreview: The black is unforgiving, but it's honest work. You float in the pilot's seat of Serenity, watching

data.passage:
The black is unforgiving, but it's honest work. You float in the pilot's seat of Serenity, watching the massive hulk of the Alliance research vessel drift against the star field like a metal corpse. Three days out from Persephone with empty cargo holds and crew getting restless about pay, this salvage job looked like a gift from the heavens.

"Ship's been dark for two weeks," Zoe reports from behind you, her voice carrying that familiar note of professional caution. "No distress beacon, no crew manifest filed with port authority."

"Which means no one's gonna come looking for whatever we find," Jayne adds with his usual charm, already checking his weapons. "I like jobs where the previous owners ain't gonna complain."

Kaylee bounces on her toes near the airlock controls, practically vibrating with excitement. "Look at the size of that thing, Cap'n! Bet she's got engine parts worth more than we've seen in months."

Simon adjusts his medical bag strap nervously. "Alliance research vessels... they don't just go dark without reason. Perhaps we should—"

"Consider the possibilities," River whispers from the shadows, her voice carrying an odd tremor. "So many voices... sleeping voices."

You study the derelict's dark viewports. Easy money's never easy, but the crew needs paying and Serenity needs fuel. The question is how to play this smart.

A.text: Take the whole crew aboard together - safety in numbers and all hands for maximum salvage
A.consequence: Crew boards together but becomes vulnerable to ship-wide threats
A.narration_directive: The entire crew boards the derelict together. Show initial exploration and discovery of research labs, but hint at the danger of having everyone in one location when things go wrong.

B.text: Send a small advance team while keeping others on Serenity as backup and escape route
B.consequence: Small team boards while maintaining backup on Serenity
B.narration_directive: Mal takes a small team (Zoe, Jayne) aboard while others remain on Serenity. This provides tactical advantage and escape route. Show methodical exploration of the derelict's outer sections.

C.text: Scan the ship thoroughly first - whatever happened over there might still be happening
C.consequence: Thorough scanning reveals concerning energy readings and life signs
C.narration_directive: Detailed scans reveal the ship's resurrection lab is still active and detect faint, impossible life signs throughout the vessel. This gives the crew crucial information before boarding.

D.text: Hail the derelict one more time before boarding - proper salvage protocol demands it
D.consequence: Hailing activates automated systems that begin hunting the crew
D.narration_directive: The hail triggers the ship's security protocols. Automated defenses come online and the ship's AI begins tracking Serenity. The derelict is no longer passive - it's now actively hostile.

- Hint transparency: subtle
- NPC forgiveness: low

- Healing speed: realistic

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PASSAGE:
The black is unforgiving, but it's honest work. You float in the pilot's seat of Serenity, watching the massive hulk of the Alliance research vessel drift against the star field like a metal corpse. Three days out from Persephone with empty cargo holds and crew getting restless about pay, this salvage job looked like a gift from the heavens.

"Ship's been dark for two weeks," Zoe reports from behind you, her voice carrying that familiar note of professional caution. "No distress beacon, no crew manifest filed with port authority."

"Which means no one's gonna come looking for whatever we find," Jayne adds with his usual charm, already checking his weapons. "I like jobs where the previous owners ain't gonna complain."

Kaylee bounces on her toes near the airlock controls, practically vibrating with excitement. "Look at the size of that thing, Cap'n! Bet she's got engine parts worth more than we've seen in months."

Simon adjusts his medical bag strap nervously. "Alliance research vessels... they don't just go dark without reason. Perhaps we should—"

"Consider the possibilities," River whispers from the shadows, her voice carrying an odd tremor. "So many voices... sleeping voices."

You study the derelict's dark viewports. Easy money's never easy, but the crew needs paying and Serenity needs fuel. The question is how to play this smart.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Take the whole crew aboard together - safety in numbers and all hands for maximum salvage
B: Send a small advance team while keeping others on Serenity as backup and escape route
C: Scan the ship thoroughly first - whatever happened over there might still be happening
D: Hail the derelict one more time before boarding - proper salvage protocol demands it

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Choice text: "Hail the derelict one more time before boarding - proper salvage protocol demands it"

OUTCOME CLASSIFICATION: SEVERE_PENALTY
Consequence: Hailing activates automated systems that begin hunting the crew
NARRATION DIRECTIVE: The hail triggers the ship's security protocols. Automated defenses come online and the ship's AI begins tracking Serenity. The derelict is no longer passive - it's now actively hostile.
You MUST narrate this outcome exactly as classified. Do not soften, alter, or provide alternatives to the predetermined outcome.

data.passage:
You key the comm with practiced ease, your voice carrying across the black. "Alliance vessel, this is Firefly-class transport Serenity. Requesting permission to come aboard for salvage operations." The words echo back from the void, professional and by-the-book.

For a heartbeat, nothing. Then the derelict's running lights flicker to life like awakening eyes. Warning klaxons begin wailing across the massive research vessel's hull, and suddenly targeting scanners are painting Serenity with search beams.

"Gorram it," you mutter, watching the tactical display light up like a Christmas tree. "Wash, get us—"

"Captain!" Kaylee's voice crackles over the comm from the engine room. "Something's got a lock on us! The whole ship just came alive!"

Zoe's at your shoulder, her expression grim. "Sir, we've got multiple weapon systems powering up on that boat. Whatever we just woke up, it ain't friendly."

Through the viewports, you can see blast shutters sliding open along the research vessel's flanks, revealing gun batteries that shouldn't exist on a civilian ship. Red warning lights strobe across its hull like a pulse, and you realize with growing dread that your simple salvage job just became a whole lot more complicated.

"Well," you say, watching death incarnate wake up in front of you, "this is new."

A.text: Order Wash to get Serenity clear of the weapons range immediately
A.consequence: Serenity escapes immediate weapons range but loses the salvage opportunity
A.narration_directive: Wash successfully pilots Serenity out of the derelict's weapons range. The crew is safe but the job is blown and they're back to square one financially.

B.text: Try to jam the derelict's targeting systems with Serenity's electronic warfare suite
B.consequence: Electronic warfare partially works but draws more attention from the ship's AI
B.narration_directive: The jamming disrupts some targeting systems but the derelict's AI adapts quickly. Serenity gains a brief window but the ship becomes more aggressive in response.

C.text: Broadcast surrender and claim you're responding to a distress beacon
C.consequence: The derelict's AI interprets surrender as confirmation of hostile intent and accelerates attack protocols
C.narration_directive: The surrender broadcast triggers additional security measures. The derelict's AI classifies Serenity as a confirmed threat and begins charging its main weapons.

D.text: Move in fast and dock before the weapons can get a clean shot
D.consequence: Risky docking maneuver succeeds but puts the crew directly aboard the hostile vessel
D.narration_directive: Wash's piloting gets Serenity docked before the weapons can fire, but now the crew is trapped aboard a ship with active defenses and no easy escape route.

{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {

    },
    "status_effect_updates": {

        {

          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "type": "string — 'condition' (default) or 'threat' (approaching dangers that fire when timer expires)",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "on_expiry": "string — REQUIRED when time_remaining is non-null. Narrative directive for what happens when timer reaches zero. Be specific and actionable.",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "modify": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to modify",
          "changes": { "severity": 0.5, "description": "updated text" },
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why this change is happening (e.g. 'Player rested, wound healing' or 'Ship accelerated, pursuit takes longer')"
        }
      ],
      "remove": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to remove",
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why removing (e.g. 'Virus deployed successfully' or 'Wound fully healed after rest')"
        }
      ]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option D.
Pre-classified outcome: SEVERE_PENALTY
Pre-determined consequence: "Hailing activates automated systems that begin hunting the crew"
Narration directive: "The hail triggers the ship's security protocols. Automated defenses come online and the ship's AI begins tracking Serenity. The derelict is no longer passive - it's now actively hostile."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

data.passagePreview: "Alliance vessel, this is the transport ship Serenity responding to your distress beacon. We surrend

data.passage:
"Alliance vessel, this is the transport ship Serenity responding to your distress beacon. We surrender and—"

The comm crackles with static before a cold, mechanical voice cuts through: "Hostile vessel identified. Surrender protocol classified as deception tactic. Initiating enhanced security measures."

"Well, that's just gorram wonderful," you mutter, watching as the derelict's weapon arrays swivel with predatory precision. Red targeting lasers dance across Serenity's hull like angry fireflies.

Zoe's knuckles are white on her rifle. "Sir, that boat's got enough firepower to turn us into space debris."

"Multiple energy signatures spiking," Kaylee calls from the engine room, her usual cheer replaced by barely controlled panic. "Mal, whatever that thing is, it's charging up something big!"

Jayne hefts Vera with grim satisfaction. "Well, least now we know where we stand."

River suddenly goes rigid, her eyes rolling back. "They're screaming," she whispers. "So many voices... all asking why they can't remember how to die."

Simon moves to comfort his sister, but his face is pale. "Mal, we need to move. Now."

The derelict's main cannon begins to glow with ominous blue light. Whatever classified nightmare lurks aboard that ship, it's decided you're a threat worth eliminating.

A.text: Order Wash to ram Serenity directly into the derelict's docking bay
A.consequence: Ramming causes structural damage to Serenity but gets the crew aboard the derelict before weapons fire
A.narration_directive: The desperate ramming maneuver succeeds but damages Serenity's hull and systems. The crew boards through the breach.

B.text: Try to hide behind the derelict's superstructure and board from the blind spot
B.consequence: Successfully uses the derelict's bulk as cover and finds an undefended docking port
B.narration_directive: Wash's piloting skills allow Serenity to slip into the derelict's sensor shadow and dock safely at an auxiliary port.

C.text: Make a desperate run for open space and hope Serenity's faster
C.consequence: The derelict's weapons track Serenity's escape attempt, causing critical engine damage
C.narration_directive: Serenity attempts to flee but the derelict's targeting systems lock on. Weapons fire cripples the engine and forces an emergency landing.

D.text: Target the derelict's weapon arrays with Serenity's small guns
D.consequence: Serenity's small guns are ineffective against the derelict's armor, and the return fire destroys the ship
D.narration_directive: Serenity's weapons barely scratch the derelict's hull. The massive ship's return fire vaporizes Serenity in a brilliant flash.

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PASSAGE:
"Alliance vessel, this is the transport ship Serenity responding to your distress beacon. We surrender and—"

The comm crackles with static before a cold, mechanical voice cuts through: "Hostile vessel identified. Surrender protocol classified as deception tactic. Initiating enhanced security measures."

"Well, that's just gorram wonderful," you mutter, watching as the derelict's weapon arrays swivel with predatory precision. Red targeting lasers dance across Serenity's hull like angry fireflies.

Zoe's knuckles are white on her rifle. "Sir, that boat's got enough firepower to turn us into space debris."

"Multiple energy signatures spiking," Kaylee calls from the engine room, her usual cheer replaced by barely controlled panic. "Mal, whatever that thing is, it's charging up something big!"

Jayne hefts Vera with grim satisfaction. "Well, least now we know where we stand."

River suddenly goes rigid, her eyes rolling back. "They're screaming," she whispers. "So many voices... all asking why they can't remember how to die."

Simon moves to comfort his sister, but his face is pale. "Mal, we need to move. Now."

The derelict's main cannon begins to glow with ominous blue light. Whatever classified nightmare lurks aboard that ship, it's decided you're a threat worth eliminating.

A: Order Wash to ram Serenity directly into the derelict's docking bay
B: Try to hide behind the derelict's superstructure and board from the blind spot
C: Make a desperate run for open space and hope Serenity's faster
D: Target the derelict's weapon arrays with Serenity's small guns

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:

data.passagePreview: The derelict's main cannon erupts in a brilliant azure lance that carves through the space where Ser

data.passage:
The derelict's main cannon erupts in a brilliant azure lance that carves through the space where Serenity hung just moments before. The blast's heat washes over your hull as Wash throws the ship into a desperate spiral, engines screaming at full burn. "We're faster than that gorram hulk!" you shout over the alarms, but even as the words leave your mouth, you see the derelict's weapon arrays swiveling with inhuman precision.

"Mal, they're tracking us!" Wash's voice cracks as secondary batteries open fire. Energy bolts pepper Serenity's stern, and the ship shudders like a wounded animal. "I can't shake 'em!"

A massive impact throws everyone against the bulkheads. Sparks cascade from overhead panels as the lights flicker and die, replaced by the hellish red glow of emergency lighting. "Engine room to bridge!" Kaylee's voice comes through static-laced with panic. "Primary engine's been hit! We're losing power fast!"

Through the viewports, you watch helplessly as the derelict's bulk grows larger. Its docking bays yawn open like hungry mouths, tractor beams reaching out to drag Serenity into its mechanical embrace. Zoe checks her weapon with grim efficiency. "Looks like we're going aboard whether we like it or not, sir."

The ship shudders again as the tractor beam takes hold.

A.text: Order the crew to prepare for hostile boarding while you try to regain control
A.consequence: Crew prepares defensive positions but boarding preparations are rushed under pressure
A.narration_directive: The crew scrambles to set up defensive positions while Mal tries emergency procedures to regain ship control. Show tension and urgency as they prepare for the worst.

B.text: Have Simon and River hide while the rest of you create a distraction
B.consequence: Simon and River are successfully hidden while the crew creates an effective distraction
B.narration_directive: Simon and River find a secure hiding spot while the rest of the crew makes noise and movement to draw attention away from them. The plan works smoothly.

C.text: Attempt to overload the engines and break free before being pulled inside
C.consequence: Engine overload attempt causes additional damage and potential crew injury from explosions
C.narration_directive: Mal orders Kaylee to overload the damaged engines. The attempt fails catastrophically, causing explosions and putting crew members in immediate danger.

D.text: Accept the situation and prepare for a tactical boarding of the derelict
D.consequence: Crew prepares for boarding but must enter the derelict under disadvantageous circumstances
D.narration_directive: Mal accepts the inevitable and orders the crew to prepare for tactical boarding. They'll enter the derelict, but on the ship's terms rather than their own.

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "status_effect_updates": {
      "add": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "type": "string — 'condition' (default) or 'threat' (approaching dangers that fire when timer expires)",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "on_expiry": "string — REQUIRED when time_remaining is non-null. Narrative directive for what happens when timer reaches zero. Be specific and actionable.",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "modify": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to modify",
          "changes": { "severity": 0.5, "description": "updated text" },
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why this change is happening (e.g. 'Player rested, wound healing' or 'Ship accelerated, pursuit takes longer')"
        }
      ],
      "remove": [
        {
          "id": "string — id of existing effect to remove",
          "update_justification": "string — REQUIRED. Why removing (e.g. 'Virus deployed successfully' or 'Wound fully healed after rest')"
        }
      ]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "location": "string — REQUIRED — current location name (e.g. The Docks, Castle Throne Room, A dark alley)",
    "time_of_day": "string — REQUIRED — one of: dawn, morning, midday, afternoon, evening, night, midnight",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky|severe_penalty|game_over|hidden_benefit|advances_act|conclusion", "consequence": "what happens mechanically", "narration_directive": "instructions for the Writer next turn" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, and npc_updates when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act, location, time_of_day.
- location: REQUIRED every turn — describe where the scene takes place (e.g. "St. Bartholomew's Church", "The Thames docks")
- time_of_day: REQUIRED every turn — set based on in-game clock: dawn (~5-7am), morning (~7-12pm), midday (~12-1pm), afternoon (~1-5pm), evening (~5-8pm), night (~8pm-12am), midnight (~12-5am)
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES PER ACT: The client enforces that acts cannot advance until scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)). Do not offer advances_act choices before this threshold.
- CRITICAL: You MUST play through ALL THREE ACTS (1, 2, 3) in order. Never skip from Act 1 directly to Act 3. Act 2 contains the rising action and character development that makes the climax meaningful.
- Do NOT set advance_act or story_complete to true on normal turns. Act advancement and story completion are now triggered by the TERMINAL CHOICE system below. Always set advance_act to false on normal turns.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD offer an advances_act choice — the act has gone on too long.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it, including advances_act choices.

TERMINAL OUTCOMES — CHOICE PRE-CLASSIFICATION:
- advances_act: Use when scenes_in_act >= MAX(3, CEIL(target_scenes * 0.5)) AND the choice would resolve the act's end_condition. At most ONE advances_act choice per set of four.
- conclusion: Only valid in Act 3. Use when the choice would resolve Act 3's end_condition and complete the story. At most ONE conclusion choice per set of four.
- game_over: Use when the choice leads to an irreversible failure appropriate to the genre (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure, etc.). At most ONE game_over choice per set of four.
- When a player selects a terminal choice, the client runs a special finale flow (GM-first, then Writer). The Writer will write a conclusive passage with NO forward-looking choices. Your choice_metadata pre-classification is the trigger.
- IMPORTANT: At most ONE terminal outcome (advances_act, conclusion, or game_over) per set of four choices. The other three choices must use normal outcomes.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effect_updates uses DELTA semantics. Only describe what CHANGED this turn. Do NOT return the full list of effects.
- add: For brand new effects only. All fields required. When the character is injured, add a status effect. Example: { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, on_expiry: "The wound closes and the pain fades to a dull ache", lethal: false }.
- on_expiry is REQUIRED for any effect with non-null time_remaining. It describes what happens narratively when the timer reaches zero. The Writer uses this to narrate the resolution. Be specific and actionable (e.g. "The virus deploys successfully, disabling Alliance tracking. River announces completion with relief.").
- modify: Target existing effects by id. Put changed fields inside "changes". update_justification is REQUIRED — explain why the change is happening.
- remove: Target effects to remove by id. update_justification is REQUIRED. Use this to resolve expired effects after the Writer has narrated their resolution.
- TIMER OWNERSHIP — HANDS OFF: The client subtracts time_elapsed from ALL timers automatically every turn. You MUST NOT use modify to adjust time_remaining unless an extraordinary, player-caused narrative event changes the timeline. Examples of VALID reasons: player cast a haste spell, player found a shortcut, player negotiated extra time. Examples of INVALID reasons: "the enemy is getting closer" (that IS the countdown), "adjusting pacing" (no — set the right duration when you create the effect), "updating threat level" (use severity, not timer). If you created an effect with a 15-minute timer, it WILL reach zero after ~15 minutes of in-game time. Trust this. Do not touch it. The system logs all timer modifications and flags unjustified overrides.
- EXPIRED EFFECTS: Effects with expired: true (visible in CURRENT PLAYER STATE) have had their timer reach zero. The Writer has been instructed to narrate their resolution using the on_expiry text. Review the Writer's passage — if the resolution was narrated adequately, issue a remove. If it wasn't, leave the effect (it will escalate to the Writer next turn).
- Use modify to adjust severity based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing. update_justification is required.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove). on_expiry should be null in this case.
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above.
- TYPE FIELD: Every status effect has a type — "condition" (default) or "threat".
  - "condition": Ongoing physical/mental states that impair or buff the player — injuries, poisons, diseases, curses, enchantments. Must have a severity that affects what the player can do.
  - "threat": Approaching dangers with a timer — pursuing enemies, structural collapse, incoming reinforcements, spreading fires. Threats MUST have a non-null time_remaining and on_expiry describing what happens when they arrive.
  - If you omit type, it defaults to "condition".
- All effects persist when their timer reaches zero until you explicitly remove them. The client never auto-removes effects.
- If nothing changed about status effects this turn, omit status_effect_updates entirely.
WHAT IS NOT A STATUS EFFECT:
- Information learned or revealed (use event_log_entry instead)
- Plot developments or narrative beats (use event_log_entry instead)
- Things that happened in the past (use event_log_entry instead)
- Static facts about the world ("Alliance knows about us") — if it doesn't change turn-to-turn and has no timer or removal condition, it's not a status effect
- Emotional reactions to events — unless they mechanically impair the character (e.g. "Paralyzed by Fear" at severity 0.7 is valid; "Feels Sad About Revelation" is not)
- Duplicate/overlapping effects — do not create a new effect when an existing one covers the same concept. Use modify to update the existing effect instead.
A status effect MUST be something that actively constrains, endangers, impairs, or buffs the player RIGHT NOW and could plausibly change or resolve. Ask: "Would removing this effect change what the player can do?" If no, it's not a status effect.

HARD MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- choice_metadata MUST include outcome, consequence, and narration_directive for every choice
- Maintain safe_choice_ratio: approximately 0.35 of choices should be advance_safe
- Lethal status effects are allowed but MUST be foreshadowed. Use add with lethal: true. There should have been clues in earlier passages.
- Lethal effects must give the player at least one turn to address them (set time_remaining in the add to allow at least one more scene).
- Full severity range (0.0-1.0). Injuries are serious and heal realistically.
- Threat-type effects should have short time windows before triggering.
- NPCs have low forgiveness. Burning a relationship has lasting consequences.
- Include at least one advance_risky or severe_penalty outcome per set of choices.
- game_over outcomes are available — use for genre-appropriate failures (death, permanent loss, catastrophic failure).
- You may use advances_act and conclusion outcomes when pacing conditions are met.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option C.
Pre-classified outcome: SEVERE_PENALTY
Pre-determined consequence: "The derelict's weapons track Serenity's escape attempt, causing critical engine damage"
Narration directive: "Serenity attempts to flee but the derelict's targeting systems lock on. Weapons fire cripples the engine and forces an emergency landing."

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- GAME_OVER: set game_over to true — the character has failed irreversibly.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
The derelict's main cannon erupts in a brilliant azure lance that carves through the space where Serenity hung just moments before. The blast's heat washes over your hull as Wash throws the ship into a desperate spiral, engines screaming at full burn. "We're faster than that gorram hulk!" you shout over the alarms, but even as the words leave your mouth, you see the derelict's weapon arrays swiveling with inhuman precision.

"Mal, they're tracking us!" Wash's voice cracks as secondary batteries open fire. Energy bolts pepper Serenity's stern, and the ship shudders like a wounded animal. "I can't shake 'em!"

A massive impact throws everyone against the bulkheads. Sparks cascade from overhead panels as the lights flicker and die, replaced by the hellish red glow of emergency lighting. "Engine room to bridge!" Kaylee's voice comes through static-laced with panic. "Primary engine's been hit! We're losing power fast!"

Through the viewports, you watch helplessly as the derelict's bulk grows larger. Its docking bays yawn open like hungry mouths, tractor beams reaching out to drag Serenity into its mechanical embrace. Zoe checks her weapon with grim efficiency. "Looks like we're going aboard whether we like it or not, sir."

The ship shudders again as the tractor beam takes hold.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Order the crew to prepare for hostile boarding while you try to regain control
B: Have Simon and River hide while the rest of you create a distraction
C: Attempt to overload the engines and break free before being pulled inside
D: Accept the situation and prepare for a tactical boarding of the derelict

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passagePreview: "Kaylee, I need you to push those engines past redline!" you bark into the comm, watching the dereli

data.passage:
"Kaylee, I need you to push those engines past redline!" you bark into the comm, watching the derelict's docking bay grow larger in the viewport. "We got maybe two minutes before they reel us in like a fish!"

"But Cap'n, the primary motivator's already cracked somethin' fierce," Kaylee's voice crackles back, strain evident even through the static. "If I overload her now—"

"Just do it!"

Zoe shoots you a look that says she's thinking the same thing Kaylee just said, but she doesn't voice it. The ship shudders as Kaylee complies, the engine room filling with the whine of overtaxed machinery.

For a heartbeat, you feel Serenity strain against the tractor beam. Then the lights flicker, alarms start screaming, and a thunderous explosion rocks the ship. The bridge consoles spark and smoke, throwing Jayne against a bulkhead as emergency lighting kicks in.

"Engine room!" Simon's voice cuts through the chaos over the comm. "Medical emergency! Kaylee's hurt!"

River suddenly goes rigid, her eyes wide with terror. "They're waking up," she whispers, barely audible over the alarms. "All of them. They hear us now."

Through the viewport, you can see Serenity being drawn inexorably toward the derelict's gaping maw of a docking bay, trailing plasma and debris from your ruined engines.

A.text: Rush to the engine room to help Kaylee while Zoe handles the bridge
A.consequence: Mal leaves the bridge unattended while helping Kaylee, potentially missing critical developments
A.narration_directive: Mal rushes to help Kaylee, leaving Zoe to handle the approaching docking. Show the engine room damage and Kaylee's condition while the ship continues toward the derelict.

B.text: Send Simon to treat Kaylee while you prepare the crew for boarding the derelict
B.consequence: Organized response to the crisis, maintaining command structure while addressing medical emergency
B.narration_directive: Mal coordinates the crew response professionally, sending Simon to treat Kaylee while preparing for the inevitable boarding. Show leadership under pressure.

C.text: Try to contact the derelict directly and demand they release your ship
C.consequence: Attempting communication triggers more aggressive derelict responses and wastes precious time
C.narration_directive: Mal's attempt to negotiate with the derelict activates more hostile automated systems and accelerates the danger to the crew.

D.text: Focus on River's warning and ask her what she's sensing from the other ship
D.consequence: River provides crucial intelligence about the derelict's reanimated inhabitants and their capabilities
D.narration_directive: Focus on River's psychic insights about the derelict. She reveals disturbing details about what's waiting aboard the ship and how to potentially avoid or combat it.
